_comment: DatumV dataset catalog — v1 manifest read by the in-app Datasets
panel and (eventually) the catalog-aware datasets.<name> SQL resolver.
Schema shape:
- Each top-level `datasets` entry is a dataset family (e.g. COCO 2017).
Entry-level fields (summary, license, attribution, card, hero)
describe the family as a whole. A `variants[]` array carries the
install-handle-bearing cuts (e.g. test2017, val2017).
- Variants own the sources, ingest jobs, version history, and size
metadata; install / uninstall / probe key on `variant.id`.
- The renderer shows one list row per entry; the detail pane renders
entry chrome (hero + card markdown) plus a variant tab strip,
flipping which variant is active without reloading the entry.
Install pipeline: download source(s) into the raw cache
(`DATUMV_DATASETS`) -> for each ingest[] job, point
DatumV.Ingestion.Ingester at the named source path so the
output `.datum` lands at <catalog>/datasets/<variantId>/<version>/<tableName>.datum.
The FormatRegistry resolves the format from the source file's
extension or magic bytes (e.g. ZipDeserializer reads a JPEG-bag
archive directly — no separate extraction step). Raw-cache contents
are deletable once ingest completes.

datasets.name: COCO 2017
datasets.summary: Microsoft Common Objects in Context — the 2017 release. Eighty-class object detection, segmentation, keypoint, and captioning corpus drawn from Flickr. The default reach-for benchmark in computer vision.
datasets.description: Every pretrained detector / segmenter / captioner reports COCO mAP, and most were trained on it. All variants share the same image distribution and label vocabulary — they differ only in which split. Pick a split, not a dataset: the schemas are interchangeable so you can swap `datasets.coco_val2017` for `datasets.coco_test2017` in a query and the only thing that changes is the number of rows.
attributions: Lin et al. (COCO consortium, 2014)
Flickr contributors (per-image rights via upstream Flickr terms)

variants.summary: 40,670 unlabeled test images. Largest split; the held-out evaluation set with no public annotations. Best for image-only experiments — pretrained detectors / captioners / depth estimators run end-to-end without an annotation pipeline.

5,000 validation images. The standard COCO benchmark slice — every mAP / BLEU / CIDEr number you see reported in a paper is computed against this split. Annotations (bbox / segmentation / captions / keypoints) ship separately and require the JSON deserializer (deferred to a follow-up); this variant is images-only for now.

118,287 training images. The largest 2017 split — used to train (or fine-tune) most COCO-pretrained detectors, segmenters, and captioners. Annotations ship separately and require the JSON deserializer (deferred to a follow-up); this variant is images-only for now. At ~18 GB it's by far the heaviest install in the family — reach for val2017 or test2017 first unless you genuinely need the training set on disk.

datasets.name: LibriSpeech
datasets.summary: 1,000 hours of read English speech derived from LibriVox audiobooks, segmented into ~10s utterances and paired with sentence-level transcripts. The reference benchmark for English ASR.
datasets.description: Every modern English speech recognizer reports word error rate against the test-clean / test-other splits. All variants share the same recording pipeline (16 kHz FLAC, sentence-aligned) and the same transcript format (one `<utterance-id> <text>` line per row in colocated `.trans.txt` files) — they differ only in speaker pool size, hours of audio, and audio quality. Pick a split: `dev-clean` / `test-clean` for noise-free held-out evaluation; `dev-other` / `test-other` for the harder, noisier eval; `train-clean-100` for a tractable training cut; `train-clean-360` and `train-other-500` to assemble the full 960-hour training corpus.
attributions: Panayotov, Chen, Povey, Khudanpur (LibriSpeech, ICASSP 2015)
LibriVox volunteer narrators (public-domain audiobook recordings)
Project Gutenberg (source texts)

variants.summary: 5.4 hours / 2,703 utterances from 40 speakers. Clean held-out development set used to tune ASR hyperparameters before reporting on test-clean.
5.3 hours / 2,864 utterances from 33 speakers. Noisier development set — recordings the alignment heuristic flagged as harder to transcribe. Used to tune robustness before reporting on test-other.
5.4 hours / 2,620 utterances from 40 speakers. The standard clean ASR evaluation slice — every WER number in the literature reports against this split. Speakers are disjoint from the training set.

5.1 hours / 2,939 utterances from 33 speakers. Noisier ASR evaluation slice paired with test-clean. WER on test-other is typically 2–3× higher than test-clean for the same model — the headline robustness number.
100.6 hours / 28,539 utterances from 251 speakers. The smallest training cut — fits in memory and trains end-to-end on a single GPU in hours. Reach for this first when prototyping an ASR recipe.
363.6 hours / 104,014 utterances from 921 speakers. Combine with train-clean-100 for the standard 460-hour clean training corpus most published recipes target.

496.7 hours / 148,688 utterances from 1,166 speakers. The noisier training cut. Combined with train-clean-100 + train-clean-360 this rounds out the full 960-hour LibriSpeech training set. ~30 GB on disk — by far the heaviest variant.

datasets.name: LJSpeech
datasets.summary: A single-speaker English speech corpus: ~24 hours of a US English female voice reading short passages from seven non-fiction LibriVox audiobooks. The canonical small-scale TTS / single-voice-ASR dataset.
datasets.description: Released in 2017 by Keith Ito, LJSpeech 1.1 is the dataset most TTS papers reach for when prototyping — small enough to fit on a laptop, clean enough to train a vocoder against in hours rather than days, and consistent enough that single-voice models converge without per-speaker conditioning. The texts are 19th- and early-20th-century non-fiction passages whose copyrights have expired; the audio was recorded by a LibriVox volunteer and dedicated to the public domain. This variant ingests audio only — the `metadata.csv` transcript file is ignored, mirroring how `coco_test2017` ingests images without annotations. Use it to validate the audio pipeline end-to-end: 13,100 short WAV clips, all 22.05 kHz mono 16-bit, no encoding surprises.
attributions: Keith Ito (LJSpeech 1.1, 2017)
LibriVox volunteer narrators (source audio)

variants.summary: 13,100 short WAV clips, ~24 hours total at 22.05 kHz mono 16-bit. Audio only — the sibling metadata.csv (transcripts + normalized text) ships in the same archive but is skipped by this variant.

datasets.name: MNIST
datasets.summary: 70,000 hand-written digits, 28×28 grayscale, paired with 0–9 labels. The reference small-scale image-classification corpus — most ML tutorials and many recipe smoke-tests reach for it first.
datasets.description: Released by LeCun, Cortes, and Burges in 1998, MNIST is built from a subset of NIST's handwriting database, normalized to 28×28 grayscale and split into 60,000 training and 10,000 test images. Every variant ships as a pair of MNIST-style IDX files — one rank-3 ubyte file of pixel data, one rank-1 ubyte file of class labels — and the install pipeline joins them on item index so each row lands with both an `image` cell and a `label` cell. Use it to validate the image pipeline end-to-end on a corpus small enough to download and ingest in under a minute on any laptop, and to sanity-check classifier training loops before scaling up to harder data.
attributions: Yann LeCun, Corinna Cortes, Christopher J.C. Burges (MNIST, 1998)
NIST (Special Database 19, source handwriting samples)

variants.summary: 60,000 training images + labels. The standard training cut used by every introductory image-classification recipe. Each row: 28×28 grayscale PNG plus the integer class label (0–9).
10,000 test images + labels. The standard held-out evaluation slice — accuracy / error-rate numbers in the literature are reported against this split.

datasets.name: Fashion-MNIST
datasets.summary: 70,000 28×28 grayscale clothing thumbnails labelled with one of ten garment categories. Drop-in replacement for MNIST — same shape, harder task.
datasets.description: Released in 2017 by Zalando Research as a more challenging counterpart to MNIST. Identical layout — two IDX files per split (rank-3 ubyte images + rank-1 ubyte labels), 60k train / 10k test, 28×28 grayscale — so any recipe / model / training loop written against MNIST runs on Fashion-MNIST unchanged. The accuracy ceiling is considerably lower (~95% test for strong CNNs vs. ~99.7% on MNIST), which makes it a better smoke-test for whether a classifier is actually learning anything. Labels 0–9 are: T-shirt/top, Trouser, Pullover, Dress, Coat, Sandal, Shirt, Sneaker, Bag, Ankle boot.
attributions: Han Xiao, Kashif Rasul, Roland Vollgraf (Fashion-MNIST, Zalando Research, 2017)

variants.summary: 60,000 training images + labels. Same shape as MNIST train — 28×28 grayscale PNG per row plus integer garment-class label.
10,000 test images + labels. Held-out evaluation slice paired with the 60k train split.

datasets.name: KMNIST
datasets.summary: 70,000 cursive Hiragana characters, 28×28 grayscale, paired with 0–9 labels. The cursive-Japanese counterpart to MNIST — drop-in replacement that swaps Latin digits for Kuzushiji characters.
datasets.description: Released in 2018 by the ROIS-CODH Center for Open Data in the Humanities (Tokyo), KMNIST takes ten Hiragana characters from pre-modern Japanese woodblock-printed books and renders 70,000 28×28 grayscale samples in the same IDX layout as MNIST. The same recipe / model / training loop written for MNIST runs on KMNIST unchanged. The challenge is qualitatively harder than MNIST — cursive Hiragana characters share more visual similarity than Latin digits — and benchmarks typically report ~95–97% test accuracy vs. ~99.7% for MNIST, making KMNIST a useful smoke-test for whether a classifier is actually learning structure rather than memorising digit shapes.
attributions: Tarin Clanuwat, Mikel Bober-Irizar, Asanobu Kitamoto, Alex Lamb, Kazuaki Yamamoto, David Ha (KMNIST, 2018)
ROIS Center for Open Data in the Humanities, Tokyo (data hosting)

variants.summary: 60,000 training images + labels. Identical IDX layout to MNIST train — 28×28 grayscale PNG per row plus integer Hiragana-class label (0–9).
10,000 test images + labels. Held-out evaluation slice paired with the 60k train split.

datasets.name: EMNIST
datasets.summary: Handwritten letters and digits in the MNIST mould — 28×28 grayscale glyphs drawn from NIST Special Database 19, paired with integer class labels. The drop-in extension that swaps MNIST's ten digits for the full alphanumeric character set.
datasets.description: Released in 2017 by Cohen, Afshar, Tapson, and van Schaik (Western Sydney University), EMNIST re-derives the MNIST pipeline from the complete NIST Special Database 19 — the same handwriting corpus MNIST sampled, but covering uppercase + lowercase letters as well as digits. Every split ships in the identical MNIST IDX layout (one rank-3 ubyte image file + one rank-1 ubyte label file), so any recipe / model / training loop written for MNIST runs on EMNIST unchanged. The one wrinkle: EMNIST stores its glyphs transposed (a 90° rotation + mirror baked into the source pixels by the NIST conversion), so the install recipe applies image_transpose() to upright each character before it lands in the table. Splits trade class count against balance: 'balanced' (47 merged classes, equal samples per class) is the reach-for default; 'byclass' is the full unbalanced 62-class set (10 digits + 26 uppercase + 26 lowercase); 'letters' is the 26-class A–Z cut with case merged. Benchmarks report ~88–91% test accuracy on balanced vs. ~99.7% on MNIST — a meaningfully harder smoke test with the same shape.
attributions: Gregory Cohen, Saeed Afshar, Jonathan Tapson, André van Schaik (EMNIST, 2017)
NIST (Special Database 19, source handwriting samples)

variants.summary: 112,800 training glyphs across 47 class-balanced categories (10 digits + 37 merged letter classes, where visually identical upper/lowercase pairs like C/c are collapsed). The reach-for EMNIST training cut — equal samples per class, so plain accuracy is a sensible headline metric. Each row: upright 28×28 grayscale PNG plus integer class label (0–46).
18,800 test glyphs across the same 47 class-balanced categories. Held-out evaluation slice paired with the balanced train split — the standard cut for reporting EMNIST accuracy.

124,800 training glyphs across 26 classes — the A–Z alphabet with upper/lowercase merged. Labels are 1-indexed (1=A … 26=Z), not 0-indexed; subtract 1 when mapping to a zero-based class array. The pure-alphabet cut for letter-only recognizers.
20,800 test glyphs across the same 26 merged-case letter classes (labels 1..26). Held-out evaluation slice paired with the letters train split.

697,932 training glyphs across the full unbalanced 62-class set: 10 digits + 26 uppercase + 26 lowercase, each as its own class (0–61). The largest EMNIST cut — class frequencies follow natural handwriting distribution, so report macro-F1 alongside accuracy. ~115 MB on the wire; by far the heaviest variant in the family.
116,323 test glyphs across the same 62 unbalanced classes (10 digits + 26 uppercase + 26 lowercase). Held-out evaluation slice paired with the byclass train split.

datasets.name: MedNIST
datasets.summary: 58,954 64×64 grayscale medical thumbnails labelled with one of six imaging modalities (AbdomenCT, BreastMRI, ChestCT, CXR, Hand, HeadCT). The medical-imaging counterpart to MNIST.
datasets.description: Compiled by Bradley Erickson (Mayo Clinic) from CT, MRI, and X-ray slices drawn from the Cancer Imaging Archive (TCIA), the RSNA Bone Age Challenge, and the NIH ChestX-ray14 release, then packaged by the MONAI team as a small drop-in benchmark for medical image classification. ~10,000 thumbnails per class (BreastMRI is slightly smaller at 8,954), all rendered to uniform 64×64 grayscale JPEG. Plays the same role for medical imaging that MNIST plays for handwritten digits — small enough to ingest in under a minute, easy enough that a tiny CNN converges to ~99% test accuracy in a few epochs, but realistic enough that the modality boundaries actually correspond to clinical categories. Folder layout is one class per directory under a top-level `MedNIST/` prefix; the class label is the folder name verbatim.
attributions: Bradley J. Erickson, MD, PhD (Mayo Clinic — MedNIST compilation)
MONAI Consortium (packaging and hosting via MONAI-extra-test-data)
The Cancer Imaging Archive (TCIA), RSNA Bone Age Challenge, NIH ChestX-ray14 (source corpora)

variants.summary: 58,954 64×64 grayscale JPEGs across six imaging modalities (AbdomenCT, BreastMRI, ChestCT, CXR, Hand, HeadCT). Each row carries the decoded image plus the modality class name extracted from the source folder.

datasets.name: EuroSAT
datasets.summary: 27,000 64×64 RGB satellite tiles from Sentinel-2 over Europe, labelled with one of ten land-use / land-cover classes. The reach-for small benchmark for remote-sensing image classification.
datasets.description: Released in 2017 by Helber et al., EuroSAT samples Sentinel-2 imagery across 34 European countries and crops it into 27,000 64×64 patches across ten classes — AnnualCrop, Forest, HerbaceousVegetation, Highway, Industrial, Pasture, PermanentCrop, Residential, River, SeaLake. The dataset ships in two forms: a 13-band multispectral version (the full Sentinel-2 sensor stack) and a 3-band RGB version. This variant ingests the RGB JPEGs. State-of-the-art models reach ~98–99% accuracy, so EuroSAT plays a similar role to MNIST in the remote-sensing space — large enough to be non-trivial, small enough to iterate on, easy enough to converge. Land cover is balanced (~2,000–3,000 samples per class) so a plain accuracy number is a reasonable headline metric.
attributions: Patrick Helber, Benjamin Bischke, Andreas Dengel, Damian Borth (EuroSAT, 2017)
European Space Agency (Sentinel-2 source imagery, Copernicus open-data terms)

variants.summary: 27,000 64×64 RGB tiles across ten land-cover classes. Each row carries the decoded JPEG image plus the class name extracted from the source folder ('Forest', 'River', …).

datasets.name: Oxford-IIIT Pets
datasets.summary: 7,349 photographs of cats and dogs across 37 breeds (~200 images per breed), with the breed encoded in each filename. The reach-for small fine-grained-classification benchmark.
datasets.description: Released in 2012 by Parkhi, Vedaldi, Zisserman, and Jawahar from Oxford's VGG group, the Oxford-IIIT Pets dataset pairs ~200 photographs per breed across 25 dog breeds and 12 cat breeds. Images vary in pose, lighting, and scale — non-trivial enough that even modern fine-grained classifiers report ~95% accuracy rather than saturating, but small enough to fit a training loop on a laptop. Filenames follow the convention <breed>_<index>.jpg; cat breeds start with a capital letter ('Abyssinian_42.jpg') and dog breeds are lowercase ('basset_hound_17.jpg'), so a single character check derives the species. The sibling annotations.tar.gz ships bounding boxes and trimap segmentation masks but is skipped by this variant — extend the recipe to a multi-archive job if you want them.
attributions: Omkar M. Parkhi, Andrea Vedaldi, Andrew Zisserman, C. V. Jawahar (Oxford-IIIT Pet Dataset, 2012)
Oxford Visual Geometry Group (data hosting)

variants.summary: 7,349 photographs across 37 cat + dog breeds. Each row carries the decoded JPEG plus the breed name extracted from the filename. ~800 MB tar.gz — by far the heaviest variant on this list.

datasets.name: CIFAR-10
datasets.summary: 60,000 32×32 RGB photographs labelled with one of ten everyday categories (airplane / automobile / bird / cat / deer / dog / frog / horse / ship / truck). The reach-for small-color benchmark after MNIST.
datasets.description: Released in 2009 by Alex Krizhevsky as part of the Tiny Images project at the University of Toronto. 60,000 32×32 RGB images split 50,000 train / 10,000 test, with exactly 6,000 images per class — so plain accuracy is a sensible headline metric. CIFAR-10 fills the slot in the benchmark ladder between MNIST (greyscale digits, ~99.7% saturated) and ImageNet (1.3 M images, multi-GPU territory): small enough to iterate on a laptop, hard enough that a strong CNN reports ~94–97% test accuracy and there's still headroom for architectural experiments. The binary distribution ships as a tar.gz containing five 10,000-record training batches and one 10,000-record test batch; each record is a 1-byte label followed by 3,072 planar RGB pixel bytes. The recipe pulls those bytes straight out of the archive via open_archive + open_cifar10 — no separate extraction step touches the raw cache.
attributions: Alex Krizhevsky (CIFAR-10, Learning Multiple Layers of Features from Tiny Images, 2009)
Antonio Torralba, Rob Fergus, William T. Freeman (Tiny Images dataset, source pool)

variants.summary: 50,000 training images + labels assembled from five 10,000-record binary batches. Each row carries a PNG-encoded 32×32 RGB image plus the integer class label (0–9).
10,000 test images + labels from the single test_batch.bin inside the upstream tar.gz. Held-out evaluation slice paired with the 50k train split.

datasets.name: CIFAR-100
datasets.summary: 60,000 32×32 RGB photographs labelled with one of 100 fine-grained classes (and one of 20 coarse-grained superclasses). The harder sibling of CIFAR-10 — same image distribution, finer label space.
datasets.description: Released alongside CIFAR-10 by Alex Krizhevsky in 2009. 60,000 32×32 RGB images split 50,000 train / 10,000 test, with exactly 600 images per fine class and a two-level taxonomy: 100 fine-grained classes grouped under 20 coarse superclasses (e.g. fine 'rose' / 'tulip' / 'sunflower' / 'orchid' / 'poppy' under coarse 'flowers'). Useful when CIFAR-10 saturates or when you want a small benchmark for hierarchical / multi-task classification. The binary distribution puts each split in a single train.bin / test.bin file; each record is 1 byte coarse label + 1 byte fine label + 3,072 planar RGB pixel bytes. The recipe surfaces both label columns so callers pick the task at query time.
attributions: Alex Krizhevsky (CIFAR-100, Learning Multiple Layers of Features from Tiny Images, 2009)
Antonio Torralba, Rob Fergus, William T. Freeman (Tiny Images dataset, source pool)

variants.summary: 50,000 training images + paired (coarse, fine) labels from train.bin. Each row carries a PNG-encoded 32×32 RGB image, the 0–19 coarse-superclass label, and the 0–99 fine-class label.
10,000 test images + paired (coarse, fine) labels from test.bin. Held-out evaluation slice paired with the 50k train split.

datasets.name: MS MARCO Passages
datasets.summary: 8,841,823 short web passages drawn from Bing query results. The reference big-text corpus for English passage-retrieval evaluation — every modern retriever (dense, sparse, hybrid) reports MRR@10 against the standard MS MARCO dev split.
datasets.description: Released by Microsoft in 2016 alongside the original MS MARCO machine-reading benchmark and reused as the corpus side of the MS MARCO Passage Ranking task (TREC Deep Learning track, BEIR, and most embedder leaderboards downstream). Each row is one passage extracted from a Bing search result — roughly one to a handful of sentences in length, averaging ~55 tokens but with a long tail of multi-paragraph passages that genuinely exercise long-context embedders like Jina. This variant ingests the corpus side only (`collection.tsv`): an integer `passage_id` plus the passage `text`. The queries + qrels splits required for a full nDCG / MRR run-loop ship as separate small TSVs upstream and are deferred to a follow-up variant. Use this corpus to build an embedding column once per passage and rank against ad-hoc natural-language queries, or as the substrate for any "semantic search over a real web corpus" demo.
attributions: Payal Bajaj, Daniel Campos, Nick Craswell, Li Deng, Jianfeng Gao, Xiaodong Liu, Rangan Majumder, Andrew McNamara, Bhaskar Mitra, Tri Nguyen, Mir Rosenberg, Xia Song, Alina Stoica, Saurabh Tiwary, Tong Wang (MS MARCO, 2016)
Microsoft Bing (source query / passage extraction pipeline)

variants.summary: 8.8M short web passages in a two-column TSV (`passage_id`, `text`). ~3 GB on disk uncompressed — by far the heaviest text install in the catalog. Embedding every passage end-to-end with MiniLM takes a few hours on CPU, under an hour on a modest GPU.

datasets.name: NFCorpus
datasets.summary: 3,633 medical/nutrition documents drawn from NutritionFacts.org, paired with 3,237 natural-language health queries and graded relevance judgments. The small, fast counterpart to MS MARCO Passages — same TextEmbedder / TextReranker shape, two orders of magnitude smaller, domain-shifted into medical IR.
datasets.description: Released in 2016 by Boteva, Gholipour, Sokolov, and Riezler (Heidelberg StatNLP group, ECIR 2016) and reused as one of the original BEIR zero-shot retrieval benchmarks. The corpus side is 3,633 long-form medical articles harvested from NutritionFacts.org — each with a title, an abstract / body text, and an integer document id; the query side is 3,237 natural-language questions a layperson might paste into a health-search box ("Does eating broccoli reduce cancer risk?"). Sizes are small enough that the whole corpus embeds end-to-end on CPU in seconds with MiniLM-L6, which makes NFCorpus the right substrate for prototyping a retrieval recipe before stepping up to MS MARCO Passages — same `passages` table shape (`passage_id`, `text`), drop-in for any embedder + cosine-ranking pipeline. This variant ingests the corpus side only; queries + qrels splits (used for headline nDCG@10 numbers in the BEIR / MTEB leaderboards) are deferred to a follow-up variant.
attributions: Vera Boteva, Demian Gholipour, Artem Sokolov, Stefan Riezler (NFCorpus — A Full-Text Learning to Rank Dataset for Medical Information Retrieval, ECIR 2016)
NutritionFacts.org / Michael Greger, MD (source articles, used under CC BY-SA 4.0)

variants.summary: 3,633 medical passages in a two-column TSV (`passage_id`, `text`). A few MB on disk; ingests in under a second and embeds end-to-end on CPU in seconds with MiniLM-L6. Reach for this first when prototyping a retrieval recipe — graduate to MS MARCO Passages once the pipeline works.

datasets.name: Quora Question Pairs
datasets.summary: 404,290 pairs of user-submitted Quora questions, each pair flagged 0/1 for whether the two questions ask the same thing. The reference benchmark for English sentence-similarity / duplicate-detection evaluation — every text embedder reports paired-cosine accuracy on this corpus.
datasets.description: Released by Quora in January 2017 alongside the Kaggle competition of the same name. Each row pairs two real Quora questions with a human-labelled `is_duplicate` flag, which makes it the canonical small benchmark for sentence embedders: encode both questions, take the cosine similarity of the unit vectors, threshold for a binary classifier, report accuracy / F1 against the gold labels. Drop-in for any of the text embedders in the model catalog — MiniLM-L6, BGE Small, Jina — because every modern English sentence embedder targets this exact shape of task during fine-tuning. The recipe ingests the canonical TSV release verbatim (six columns: id, qid1, qid2, question1, question2, is_duplicate); embedding-friendly variants that explode the table into one question per row, or that materialize positive / negative / hard-negative triplets, are deferred to follow-up recipes.
attributions: Shankar Iyer, Nikhil Dandekar, Kornél Csernai (Quora — First Quora Dataset Release: Question Pairs, January 2017)
Quora users (source questions)

variants.summary: 404,290 question pairs in the canonical six-column TSV. ~55 MB on disk; ingests in seconds and embeds end-to-end on CPU in a few minutes with MiniLM-L6.

datasets.name: TweetEval Sentiment
datasets.summary: ~60,000 short English tweets labelled negative / neutral / positive — the sentiment subtask of TweetEval (Cardiff NLP, 2020), itself a repackaging of the SemEval-2017 Task 4 sentiment-in-Twitter corpus. The reference small-text classification benchmark; drop-in for any TextClassifier recipe.
datasets.description: Released in 2020 by Barbieri, Camacho-Collados, Espinosa-Anke, and Neves (Cardiff NLP, EMNLP Findings) as a unified seven-task benchmark for tweet classification. The sentiment subtask is the SemEval-2017 Task 4 corpus repackaged into a single English three-class problem (negative / neutral / positive) over ~60,000 tweets. Each row is one short tweet plus an integer label — text bodies are pre-redacted (URLs replaced with 'http', user mentions with '@user'), so the parquet is safe to embed and classify without a privacy preprocessing step. All three splits share the same `text` / `label` schema, so swap `datasets.tweeteval_sentiment_train` for `datasets.tweeteval_sentiment_test` in a query and the only thing that changes is the number of rows. The data is licensed CC BY 3.0 by the SemEval-2017 organizers and points directly at Cardiff NLP's upstream HuggingFace repo at a pinned revision — DatumV is not redistributing the tweet bodies. The underlying tweets remain subject to Twitter's Terms of Service and the Twitter API Developer Agreement; downstream redistribution or production-facing use should account for that layered constraint independently.
attributions: Francesco Barbieri, Jose Camacho-Collados, Luis Espinosa-Anke, Leonardo Neves (TweetEval: Unified Benchmark and Comparative Evaluation for Tweet Classification, EMNLP Findings 2020)
Sara Rosenthal, Noura Farra, Preslav Nakov (SemEval-2017 Task 4: Sentiment Analysis in Twitter — source corpus, CC BY 3.0)
Twitter users (source tweets, subject to Twitter Terms of Service and Twitter API Developer Agreement)

variants.summary: 45,615 training tweets + integer sentiment labels (0=negative, 1=neutral, 2=positive). The standard training cut — fine-tunes a small encoder (DistilBERT / MiniLM / Twitter-RoBERTa) on a single GPU in minutes or on CPU in tens of minutes.
2,000 validation tweets + integer labels. The smallest split — used to tune hyperparameters before reporting on test.
12,284 test tweets + integer labels. The held-out evaluation slice — every accuracy / macro-F1 number reported on the TweetEval sentiment leaderboard is computed against this split.

datasets.name: Jigsaw Toxic Comments
datasets.summary: 159,571 Wikipedia talk-page comments labelled with six binary toxicity flags (toxic, severe_toxic, obscene, threat, insult, identity_hate). The reference small benchmark for English multi-label toxicity classification — the original 2017–2018 Kaggle competition corpus that every content-moderation classifier reports against.
datasets.description: Released by Jigsaw (Google) in late 2017 for the Kaggle 'Toxic Comment Classification Challenge'. Each row is one Wikipedia talk-page comment paired with six independent 0/1 labels — comments can carry any combination of flags, including none. The training cut is 159,571 rows; the held-out test cut is 153,164 rows of which roughly 63,978 (the rows whose labels are not `-1`) were used for the official private-leaderboard scoring — the rest were dropped from evaluation and ship with `-1` sentinels in `test_labels.csv`. The recipe joins `test.csv` and `test_labels.csv` on the `id` column and filters out the `-1`-bearing rows so the resulting table holds only the scoring slice. Jigsaw released the corpus under CC0 on the competition page; the underlying comment text comes from Wikipedia talk-page revision history. Reach for it as the small-but-real text-classification benchmark — fits in memory, embeds end-to-end in minutes with MiniLM-L6, and gives a multi-label F1 number directly comparable to the Kaggle leaderboard.
attributions: Jigsaw / Google (Toxic Comment Classification Challenge, Kaggle 2017–2018)
Wikipedia talk-page contributors (source comment text)

variants.summary: 159,571 labelled training comments in an eight-column CSV (`id`, `comment_text`, plus six 0/1 label columns). ~25 MB gzipped on the wire; ingests in seconds and embeds end-to-end on CPU in a few minutes with MiniLM-L6.

63,978 scored test comments after joining `test.csv` (the held-out comment text) with `test_labels.csv` (post-competition gold labels) on `id` and filtering out rows whose labels are `-1` (excluded from official private-leaderboard scoring). Use as the canonical held-out evaluation slice paired with the 160k train split.

datasets.name: Chicago Crimes
datasets.summary: 8M+ Chicago Police Department incident reports from 2001 to the present. The reach-for big-tabular benchmark for time-series, geospatial, and categorical analytics over a single municipal stream.
datasets.description: Published by the Chicago Police Department through the City of Chicago Open Data Portal (Socrata-hosted). Each row is one reported incident: timestamp, block-level address, FBI / IUCR offense codes, arrest + domestic flags, lat/lng (Socrata rounds to the city block to protect privacy), and the beat / district / ward / community-area administrative codes. The corpus refreshes daily with a roughly 7-day reporting lag, so the headline row count and on-disk size drift over time. The ingest recipe drives `open_csv_typed` at plan time so the schema lands with real per-column types (TIMESTAMPTZ for the date columns, FLOAT for lat/lng, narrowed integers for the FBI code) rather than the `Array<String>` shape `open_csv` yields — queries can write `WHERE "Primary Type" = 'THEFT' AND "Year" = 2024` against typed columns directly.
attributions: Chicago Police Department (incident report data)
City of Chicago Open Data Portal (hosting + Socrata distribution)

variants.summary: Direct CSV from the City of Chicago Open Data Portal. Counts and bytes drift with each daily update — reach for this variant for freshness-sensitive work (recent-trend dashboards, current-quarter analysis) rather than reproducible benchmarks. As of mid-2026, ~8.2M rows and ~1.8 GB on the wire; both grow on the order of 5k–10k rows per day.

datasets.name: SEC EDGAR Filings Index
datasets.summary: Every regulatory filing the SEC received between 2021 Q1 and 2025 Q4 — roughly 7 million 10-Ks, 10-Qs, 8-Ks, S-1s, and the long tail of less-famous form types — landed as one unified `edgar_filings` table with typed CIK, form-type, and date-filed columns. Reach-for big-tabular substrate for company-level, time-windowed, and form-type queries against a real regulatory firehose.
datasets.description: Published by the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission under public-domain access at https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/full-index/. Each quarter's `master.idx` is a pipe-delimited filings list — CIK | Company Name | Form Type | Date Filed | Filename — that the recipe parses with `open_csv` (delimiter='|'), filters via `regexp_like(fields[0], '^[0-9]+$')` to drop the preamble + header + dashes lines, types the CIK as Int32 and the filed date as DATE, and stamps each row with a `quarter` literal so cross-quarter queries can group by era. The Filename column points at the actual filing document under `https://www.sec.gov/Archives/<filename>` — not ingested here (those are megabytes apiece), but the index is the join key for fetching them later. SEC's access policy requires a contact-bearing User-Agent on every request; the source declares one via the per-source `userAgent` field. The window stops at 2025 Q4 to give a clean 5-year boundary; if you want deeper history reach back through 1993 Q1, the source URL pattern just changes the year folder.
attributions: U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (EDGAR dissemination feed)

variants.summary: Twenty quarterly master.idx files pulled fresh from www.sec.gov, unioned through a single ingest recipe into one `edgar_filings` table. ~660 MB on the wire, ~7M rows, ~150–200 MB ingested footprint after columnar typing. The 2021–2025 window is a clean five-year cut — recent enough that current-day queries land against familiar companies, deep enough to cover the COVID-era SPAC boom and bust.
